1:
"And it came to pass on the eighth day, that Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel;" The very brief verse notes the eighth day, the priesthood entering on its ministry. The grammatical voices note the day. Henry and Gill note that "it came to pass on the eighth day, that Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel" — on the eighth day (after the seven days of consecration, Leviticus 8:33-35), Moses called Aaron, his sons, and the elders, that the priesthood might begin its public ministry; the eighth day (the day after the week of consecration, the first day of the priests' ministry); the calling (Moses summoning the priests and the elders to the inauguration of the priesthood); the figure (the eighth day, the new beginning, the day after the sabbath, the figure of a new order, perhaps of the resurrection, the eighth day of new creation).

On "it came to pass on the eighth day," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the new beginning. Calvin and Ellicott note the eighth day (after the seven of consecration, the priesthood now entering its work, the new beginning, Leviticus 8:33) — the figure (the eighth day, the day of new beginning, the priesthood entering its ministry).

On "Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the calling: "Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel" — the priests and elders called (the inauguration of the priesthood, the people represented by the elders); the providence (the priesthood beginning its ministry, the people gathered). The verse displays the eighth day, the priesthood entering on its ministry.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the day — "it came to pass on the eighth day, that Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel," the new beginning (Leviticus 8:33-35, Henry, Gill); "it came to pass on the eighth day," the new beginning (Calvin, Ellicott); "Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel," the calling (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the eighth day. The verse marks the day: the eighth. The verse displays the priesthood: beginning. The verse displays the calling: the priests and elders. The verse displays the ministry: begun. The day displays the priesthood entering its ministry. The verse displays the eighth day: the new beginning. The verse displays the priests: called. The verse displays the ministry: inaugurated. The eighth day, the priesthood entering on its ministry, displays the new beginning: on the eighth day Moses called Aaron and his sons (Leviticus 8:33-35) — the day after the consecration, the priesthood entering on its public ministry. The verse displays the offerings commanded (vv.2-4).
2:
"And he said unto Aaron, Take thee a young calf for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish, and offer them before the LORD." The very brief verse notes the offerings commanded for Aaron himself. The grammatical voices note the offerings. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he said unto Aaron, Take thee a young calf for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish, and offer them before the LORD" — Aaron was to take a young calf for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering for himself, both without blemish; the calf for a sin offering (the atonement for Aaron's own sin, perhaps a reminder of the golden calf, Exodus 32); the ram for a burnt offering (the whole devotion); the without blemish (the unblemished victims, the figure of Christ, 1 Peter 1:19); the for himself (Aaron offering for his own sin first, vv.7).

On "Take thee a young calf for a sin offering," the witnesses note the calf. Gill notes the calf for Aaron's sin offering (the atonement for himself, perhaps recalling the calf he made, Exodus 32) — the figure (the priest's own sin needing atonement, Hebrews 5:3).

On "a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the burnt offering: "a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish, and offer them before the LORD" — the ram for the burnt offering (the whole devotion, after the atonement); the figure (the unblemished victims, Christ, 1 Peter 1:19); the providence (Aaron's offerings for himself, the atonement and devotion). The verse displays the offerings commanded for Aaron himself.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offerings — "Take thee a young calf for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering... and offer them before the LORD," the offerings (Exodus 32, Keil, Gill); "Take thee a young calf for a sin offering," the calf (Hebrews 5:3, Gill); "a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish," the burnt offering (1 Peter 1:19, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Aaron's offerings. The verse marks the calf: the sin offering. The verse displays the ram: the burnt offering. The verse displays the atonement: for himself. The verse displays the Christ: the unblemished. The offerings display Aaron's atonement and devotion. The verse displays the calf: for sin. The verse displays the ram: for devotion. The verse displays the Christ: without blemish. The offerings commanded for Aaron himself display the priest's own atonement: a calf for a sin offering, a ram for a burnt offering (Exodus 32; Hebrews 5:3) — Aaron's atonement and devotion for himself, the unblemished victims, the figure of Christ. The verse displays the offerings for the people (vv.3-4).
3:
"And unto the children of Israel thou shalt speak, saying, Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering; and a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering;" The very brief verse notes the offerings commanded for the people. The grammatical voices note the offerings. JFB and Gill note that "unto the children of Israel thou shalt speak... Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering; and a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering" — the people were to bring a kid of the goats for a sin offering, and a calf and a lamb for a burnt offering, all without blemish; the kid for a sin offering (the atonement for the people); the calf and lamb for a burnt offering (the whole devotion); the without blemish (the unblemished victims, the figure of Christ); the for the people (the offerings for the congregation, after Aaron's own, vv.7).

On "Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering," the witnesses note the sin offering. Gill notes the kid for the people's sin offering (the atonement for the congregation) — the figure (the atonement for the people).

On "a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the burnt offering: "a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering" — the calf and lamb for the burnt offering (the whole devotion of the people); the figure (the unblemished victims, Christ); the providence (the people's offerings, the atonement and devotion). The verse displays the offerings commanded for the people.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offerings — "Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering; and a calf and a lamb... for a burnt offering," the offerings (JFB, Gill); "Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering," the sin offering (Gill); "a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering," the burnt offering (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the people's offerings. The verse marks the kid: the sin offering. The verse displays the calf and lamb: the burnt offering. The verse displays the atonement: for the people. The verse displays the Christ: the unblemished. The offerings display the people's atonement and devotion. The verse displays the kid: for sin. The verse displays the burnt offering: devotion. The verse displays the Christ: without blemish. The offerings commanded for the people display the atonement and devotion: a kid for a sin offering, a calf and a lamb for a burnt offering — the people's atonement and whole devotion, the unblemished victims, the figure of Christ. The verse displays the peace offerings and the meal offering (vv.4-5).
4:
"Also a bullock and a ram for peace offerings, to sacrifice before the LORD; and a meat offering mingled with oil: for to day the LORD will appear unto you." The witnesses note the peace and meal offerings, and the promise of the LORD's appearing. On "a bullock and a ram for peace offerings... and a meat offering mingled with oil," the grammatical voices note the offerings. Ellicott and Gill note that "a bullock and a ram for peace offerings, to sacrifice before the LORD; and a meat offering mingled with oil: for to day the LORD will appear unto you" — the people were also to bring a bullock and a ram for peace offerings, and a meal offering mingled with oil; the peace offerings (the fellowship and communion with God); the meal offering (the devotion, fine flour and oil); the full round of offerings (sin, burnt, peace, meal — the whole system, chs. 1-7).

On "for to day the LORD will appear unto you," interpreters expound the promise. The reformed and the dominant reading note the appearing — "for to day the LORD will appear unto you": the great promise, that on this day, when the offerings were rightly made, the LORD would appear (his glory, vv.23-24); the witnesses note the significance — the LORD's appearing the goal of the worship (the offerings made that God might manifest his presence and acceptance, the glory appearing, vv.23-24); the lesson — the manifest presence of God the reward and end of right worship (God meeting his people in the appointed way, the glory revealed); the figure (the appearing of God's glory, the acceptance of the offerings; ultimately the appearing of God in Christ, John 1:14; and the hope of his appearing in glory, Titus 2:13; Hebrews 9:28).

On the appearing, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the goal. Wesley notes the LORD appearing the goal of the offerings (the glory to be manifested) — the providence (the worship that God might appear, the glory, vv.23-24). The verse displays the peace and meal offerings, and the promise of the LORD's appearing.

The witnesses agree the substance: the peace and meal offerings were brought, with the promise that the LORD would appear (Ellicott, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the promise of the LORD's appearing — the LORD's manifest presence the goal and reward of right worship (the offerings rightly made that God might appear, the glory revealed, vv.23-24), a figure of the appearing of God in Christ (John 1:14) and the hope of his appearing in glory (Titus 2:13; Hebrews 9:28). The verse displays the appearing-promise. The verse marks the offerings: peace and meal. The verse displays the appearing: promised. The verse displays the glory: to be revealed. The verse displays the Christ: God appearing. The promise displays the LORD's appearing the goal of worship. The verse displays the offerings: full. The verse displays the LORD: to appear. The verse displays the glory: the goal. The peace and meal offerings, and the promise of the LORD's appearing, display the goal of worship: to day the LORD will appear unto you (vv.23-24; John 1:14; Titus 2:13) — the LORD's manifest presence the reward of right worship, a figure of God appearing in Christ and the hope of his glory. The verse displays the people drawing near (vv.5-6).
5:
"And they brought that which Moses commanded before the tabernacle of the congregation: and all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD." The very brief verse notes the congregation drawing near before the LORD. The grammatical voices note the gathering. Ellicott and Gill note that "they brought that which Moses commanded before the tabernacle of the congregation: and all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD" — the people brought the commanded offerings and drew near, standing before the LORD; the bringing (the offerings brought as commanded); the drawing near (the congregation approaching, standing before God); the standing before the LORD (the people gathered for worship, awaiting the LORD's appearing); the figure (the congregation drawing near to God, the access through the offerings, Hebrews 10:22, "let us draw near").

On "they brought that which Moses commanded," the witnesses note the obedience. Gill notes the offerings brought as commanded (the obedience) — the obedience (the people doing as commanded).

On "all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD," the grammatical-historical note the drawing near: "all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD" — the congregation approaching, standing before God (the worship, the access); the figure (the drawing near to God through the offerings, Hebrews 10:22); the providence (the people gathered, awaiting the LORD's appearing). The verse displays the congregation drawing near before the LORD.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the gathering — "they brought that which Moses commanded... and all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD," the drawing near (Hebrews 10:22, Ellicott, Gill); "they brought that which Moses commanded," the obedience (Gill); "all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD," the drawing near (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered people. The verse marks the offerings: brought. The verse displays the congregation: drawing near. The verse displays the standing: before the LORD. The verse displays the access: through the offerings. The gathering displays the people drawing near to God. The verse displays the people: approaching. The verse displays the worship: gathered. The verse displays the access: through offerings. The congregation drawing near before the LORD displays the access: they drew near and stood before the LORD (Hebrews 10:22) — the people drawing near to God through the offerings, gathered for worship, awaiting the LORD's appearing. The verse displays Moses' word of the LORD's appearing (v.6).
6:
"And Moses said, This is the thing which the LORD commanded that ye should do: and the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you." The witnesses note the promise that the glory of the LORD shall appear — the crux of the goal of worship. On "This is the thing which the LORD commanded that ye should do: and the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you," interpreters expound the promise. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the appearing — "the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you": Moses declared that, when they did as the LORD commanded, the glory of the LORD would appear; the witnesses note the significance — the glory of the LORD the goal and reward of obedient worship (the manifest presence of God, the visible glory, to appear when the offerings were rightly made, vv.23-24); the lesson — the connection of obedience and the manifest presence (the glory appears to those who do as God commands, the worship in God's appointed way crowned with his manifest presence; "this is the thing which the LORD commanded... and the glory shall appear" — obedience the way to the manifest glory); the figure — the glory of God revealed to the obedient, the manifest presence the end of worship, fulfilled in the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6), and the hope of beholding his glory (John 17:24); the encouragement (the assurance that God would manifest his acceptance and presence).

On "This is the thing which the LORD commanded that ye should do," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the obedience. Wesley and Ellicott note the doing as commanded (the obedience, the worship in God's way) — the obedience (the appointed worship, the condition of the glory appearing).

On the glory, the grammatical-historical note the appearing: the glory shall appear — the manifest presence; the providence (the glory the goal of the obedient worship, vv.23-24). The verse displays the promise that the glory of the LORD shall appear.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses promised that, when they obeyed, the glory of the LORD would appear (Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the goal of worship: "the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you" — the manifest presence of God the goal and reward of obedient worship (the glory to appear when the offerings were rightly made, vv.23-24), the connection of obedience and the manifest presence, a figure of the glory of God revealed in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6) and the hope of beholding his glory (John 17:24). The verse displays the glory-promise crux. The verse marks the command: do this. The verse displays the glory: shall appear. The verse displays the obedience: the way. The verse displays the Christ: the glory revealed. The promise displays the glory the goal of obedient worship. The verse displays the obedience: required. The verse displays the glory: promised. The verse displays the Christ: the face of glory. The promise that the glory of the LORD shall appear displays the crux: do this, and the glory of the LORD shall appear (vv.23-24; 2 Corinthians 4:6; John 17:24) — the manifest presence of God the goal of obedient worship, a figure of the glory revealed in Christ. The verse displays Aaron's first offering (vv.7-14).
7:
"And Moses said unto Aaron, Go unto the altar, and offer thy sin offering, and thy burnt offering, and make an atonement for thyself, and for the people: and offer the offering of the people, and make an atonement for them; as the LORD commanded." The witnesses note Aaron offering for himself first, then the people — the crux of the priest's own need. On "offer thy sin offering, and thy burnt offering, and make an atonement for thyself, and for the people," interpreters expound the order. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the priest's own offering — "make an atonement for thyself, and for the people": Aaron was to offer first for himself (his own sin offering and burnt offering), making atonement for himself, and then for the people; the witnesses note the significance — the priest must make atonement for his own sin before he can offer for the people (Aaron himself a sinner, needing atonement first); the lesson — the Aaronic high priest's own sinfulness (the priest a sinner like the people, needing to offer first for himself, the imperfection of the Levitical priesthood); the contrast with Christ — the great difference from Christ the sinless High Priest, who needed no offering for himself (Hebrews 7:26-27, "such an high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners... who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's: for this he did once, when he offered up himself"; Hebrews 5:3, the Aaronic priest "ought... to offer for sins, as for the people, so also for himself"); the figure — the imperfect priest pointing to the perfect, the sinful mediator to the sinless.

On "and offer the offering of the people, and make an atonement for them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the people's atonement. Wesley and Keil note Aaron then offering for the people (the atonement for the congregation, after his own) — the order (himself first, then the people, the priest cleansed before interceding); the providence (the atonement for the people, the priest's mediation). The verse displays Aaron offering for himself first, then the people.

The witnesses agree the substance: Aaron offered first for himself, then for the people, making atonement for both (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the priest's own need: "make an atonement for thyself, and for the people" — the Aaronic high priest must offer first for his own sin (himself a sinner, needing atonement before he can mediate for the people), the imperfection of the Levitical priesthood, in sharp contrast with Christ the sinless High Priest who needed no offering for himself but offered up himself once for all (Hebrews 7:26-27; 5:3), the imperfect priest pointing to the perfect. The verse displays the priest's-own-need crux. The verse marks the atonement: for thyself first. The verse displays the priest: a sinner. The verse displays the people: then atoned. The verse displays the Christ: the sinless Priest. The order displays the priest's own need before the people's. The verse displays the Aaron: needing atonement. The verse displays the Christ: needing none. The verse displays the priesthood: imperfect. Aaron offering for himself first, then the people, displays the crux: make an atonement for thyself, and for the people (Hebrews 7:26-27; 5:3) — the Aaronic priest a sinner needing atonement first, in contrast with Christ the sinless High Priest who offered up himself. The verse displays the slaying of Aaron's calf (vv.8-11).
8:
"Aaron therefore went unto the altar, and slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself." The very brief verse notes Aaron slaying his own sin offering. The grammatical voices note the slaying. Henry and Gill note that "Aaron therefore went unto the altar, and slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself" — Aaron went to the altar and slew the calf of his own sin offering; the going to the altar (Aaron beginning his priestly ministry); the slaying (the death of the victim, the atonement for himself); the for himself (the sin offering for Aaron's own sin, vv.7); the figure (the priest's own atonement, the imperfect priest; the calf perhaps recalling the golden calf, Exodus 32).

On "Aaron therefore went unto the altar," the witnesses note the ministry. Gill notes Aaron going to the altar (beginning his ministry, the first sacrifice) — the ministry (Aaron entering on his priestly work).

On "slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself," the grammatical-historical note the atonement: "slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself" — the calf slain for Aaron's own sin (the atonement for himself, vv.7); the figure (the priest's own sin needing atonement, Hebrews 5:3); the providence (Aaron's atonement, the priest cleansed first). The verse displays Aaron slaying his own sin offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the slaying — "Aaron therefore went unto the altar, and slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself," the atonement (vv.7, Henry, Gill); "Aaron therefore went unto the altar," the ministry (Gill); "slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself," the atonement (Hebrews 5:3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Aaron's slaying. The verse marks the altar: approached. The verse displays the calf: slain. The verse displays the atonement: for himself. The verse displays the priest: a sinner. The slaying displays Aaron's own atonement. The verse displays the Aaron: ministering. The verse displays the calf: slain. The verse displays the priest: cleansed first. Aaron slaying his own sin offering displays the priest's own atonement: he slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself (vv.7; Hebrews 5:3) — Aaron beginning his ministry with the atonement for his own sin, the imperfect priest cleansed first. The verse displays the blood and the burning (vv.9-11).
9:
"And the sons of Aaron brought the blood unto him: and he dipped his finger in the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar, and poured out the blood at the bottom of the altar:" The very brief verse notes the blood of Aaron's sin offering applied to the altar. The grammatical voices note the blood. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the sons of Aaron brought the blood unto him: and he dipped his finger in the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar, and poured out the blood at the bottom of the altar" — the blood of Aaron's sin offering was put on the horns of the altar and poured at its base; the sons bringing the blood (the priests assisting); the blood on the horns (the atonement applied to the altar); the poured out at the base (the rest of the blood, the full giving of the life); the figure (the blood the means of atonement, the life poured out, Christ).

On "he dipped his finger in the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar," the witnesses note the application. Gill notes the blood on the horns of the altar (the atonement applied, Leviticus 4:25) — the figure (the blood of atonement).

On "poured out the blood at the bottom of the altar," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the pouring: "poured out the blood at the bottom of the altar" — the rest of the blood poured (the full giving of the life); the figure (the life poured out, Isaiah 53:12); the providence (the blood applied and poured, the atonement). The verse displays the blood of Aaron's sin offering applied to the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the applied blood. The verse marks the blood: on the horns. The verse displays the pouring: at the base. The verse displays the atonement: applied. 10:
"But the fat, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver of the sin offering, he burnt upon the altar; as the LORD commanded Moses." The very brief verse notes the fat of Aaron's sin offering burned. The grammatical voices note the fat. Ellicott and Gill note that "the fat, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver of the sin offering, he burnt upon the altar; as the LORD commanded Moses" — the fat, kidneys, and caul of Aaron's sin offering were burned on the altar, as commanded; the fat burned (the best given to God, Leviticus 4:8-10); the inward parts (the kidneys and caul, the seat of the affections); the as commanded (the exact obedience); the figure (the best given to God, the inward devotion, even in the sin offering).

On "the fat... of the sin offering, he burnt upon the altar," the witnesses note the fat. Gill notes the fat burned to God (the best, Leviticus 4:8) — the figure (the best, the inward, given to God).

On "as the LORD commanded Moses," the grammatical-historical note the obedience: "as the LORD commanded Moses" — the exact obedience (the fat burned as commanded); the figure (the obedience in the offering); the providence (the fat burned, the obedience kept). The verse displays the fat of Aaron's sin offering burned.

Ellicott and Gill note that "the flesh and the hide he burnt with fire without the camp" — the flesh and hide of Aaron's sin offering were burned outside the camp; the body burned without the camp (the sin offering for the priest wholly burned outside, Leviticus 4:11-12); the not eaten (the priest's own sin offering not eaten by himself); the figure (the body burned without the camp, the figure of Christ suffering without the gate, Hebrews 13:11-13).

The verse displays the body of Aaron's sin offering burned without the camp.

12:
"And he slew the burnt offering; and Aaron's sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled round about upon the altar." The very brief verse notes the slaying of Aaron's burnt offering and the sprinkling of its blood. The grammatical voices note the burnt offering. Ellicott and Gill note that "he slew the burnt offering; and Aaron's sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled round about upon the altar" — Aaron slew the ram of his burnt offering, and his sons presented the blood, which he sprinkled round about on the altar; the burnt offering (the whole offering of devotion, after the sin offering); the slaying (the death of the victim); the blood sprinkled round about (the atonement applied on every side); the figure (the burnt offering, the whole devotion, after the atonement; the blood, the atonement).

The verse displays the slaying of Aaron's burnt offering and the sprinkling of its blood.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 13:
"And they presented the burnt offering unto him, with the pieces thereof, and the head: and he burnt them upon the altar." The very brief verse notes the burning of the pieces of Aaron's burnt offering. The grammatical voices note the burning. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they presented the burnt offering unto him, with the pieces thereof, and the head: and he burnt them upon the altar" — the burnt offering, cut in pieces with the head, was presented and burned on the altar; the pieces and the head (the victim cut up, all burned, Leviticus 1:6-9); the burned (the whole offering consumed, the whole devotion); the figure (the whole offering wholly burned, Christ wholly given, Ephesians 5:2).

On "they presented the burnt offering unto him, with the pieces thereof, and the head," the witnesses note the pieces. Gill notes the pieces and head presented (the victim cut up, Leviticus 1:8) — the whole offering (all burned).

On "he burnt them upon the altar," the grammatical-historical note the burning: "he burnt them upon the altar" — the whole burned (the whole offering consumed); the figure (the whole devotion, Christ wholly given, Ephesians 5:2); the providence (the burnt offering wholly burned). The verse displays the burning of the pieces of Aaron's burnt offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the burning — "they presented the burnt offering unto him, with the pieces thereof, and the head: and he burnt them upon the altar," the pieces burned (Leviticus 1:6-9, Keil, Gill); "they presented the burnt offering unto him, with the pieces thereof, and the head," the pieces (Gill); "he burnt them upon the altar," the burning (Ephesians 5:2, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the burnt pieces. The verse marks the pieces: presented. The verse displays the head: with them. The verse displays the offering: wholly burned. The verse displays the Christ: wholly given. The burning displays the whole offering consumed. The verse displays the pieces: burned. The verse displays the offering: whole. The verse displays the Christ: the offering. The burning of the pieces of Aaron's burnt offering displays the whole devotion: the pieces and head burned (Leviticus 1:6-9; Ephesians 5:2) — the whole offering consumed, the figure of Christ wholly given. The verse displays the washing and burning of the inwards (v.14).
14:
"And he did wash the inwards and the legs, and burnt them upon the burnt offering on the altar." The very brief verse notes the washing and burning of the inwards. The grammatical voices note the washing. Ellicott and Gill note that "he did wash the inwards and the legs, and burnt them upon the burnt offering on the altar" — the inwards and legs of the burnt offering were washed and burned on the altar; the washing (the inwards and legs cleansed before burning, Leviticus 1:9); the burned (added to the burnt offering, the whole consumed); the figure (the washing, the purity required; the inwards, the inner man; the legs, the walk — the inner and outer cleansed and offered to God).

On "he did wash the inwards and the legs," the witnesses note the washing. Gill notes the inwards and legs washed (cleansed before burning, Leviticus 1:9) — the figure (the purity, the inner and outer cleansed).

On "burnt them upon the burnt offering on the altar," the grammatical-historical note the burning: "burnt them upon the burnt offering on the altar" — the washed parts burned (added to the offering, the whole consumed); the figure (the whole, inner and outer, offered to God); the providence (the burnt offering complete). The verse displays the washing and burning of the inwards.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the washing — "he did wash the inwards and the legs, and burnt them upon the burnt offering on the altar," the washing and burning (Leviticus 1:9, Ellicott, Gill); "he did wash the inwards and the legs," the washing (Gill); "burnt them upon the burnt offering on the altar," the burning (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the washed inwards. The verse marks the inwards: washed. The verse displays the legs: cleansed. The verse displays the burning: on the altar. The verse displays the purity: required. The washing displays the inner and outer cleansed and offered. The verse displays the inwards: washed. The verse displays the burning: complete. The verse displays the offering: whole. The washing and burning of the inwards display the purity and whole devotion: the inwards and legs washed and burned (Leviticus 1:9) — the inner and outer cleansed and offered to God, the whole burnt offering complete. The verse displays the offerings for the people (vv.15-21).
15:
"And he brought the people's offering, and took the goat, which was the sin offering for the people, and slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first." The very brief verse notes Aaron offering the people's sin offering. The grammatical voices note the offering. Ellicott and Gill note that "he brought the people's offering, and took the goat, which was the sin offering for the people, and slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first" — having offered for himself, Aaron then brought the people's offering, slaying the goat of their sin offering, as he had done his own; the people's offering (after his own, the order, vv.7); the goat (the people's sin offering); the as the first (offered in the same manner as his own sin offering); the figure (the atonement for the people, after the priest's own; the order of mediation).

On "he brought the people's offering, and took the goat," the witnesses note the people's atonement. Gill notes the goat of the people's sin offering (the atonement for the congregation, after Aaron's own, vv.7) — the order (himself first, then the people).

On "slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first," the grammatical-historical note the manner: "slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first" — the people's sin offering offered as Aaron's own (the same manner); the figure (the atonement for the people, the priest's mediation); the providence (the people's atonement, after the priest's own). The verse displays Aaron offering the people's sin offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offering — "he brought the people's offering, and took the goat... and slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first," the people's atonement (vv.7, Ellicott, Gill); "he brought the people's offering, and took the goat," the people's offering (Gill); "slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first," the manner (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the people's sin offering. The verse marks the people: their offering. The verse displays the goat: slain. The verse displays the atonement: for the people. The verse displays the order: after his own. The offering displays the atonement for the people. The verse displays the goat: offered. The verse displays the atonement: made. The verse displays the priest: mediating. Aaron offering the people's sin offering displays the atonement for the people: he took the goat, the sin offering for the people, and offered it (vv.7) — Aaron, having atoned for himself, now making atonement for the people, the priest's mediation. The verse displays the burnt and meal offerings for the people (vv.16-17).
16:
"And he brought the burnt offering, and offered it according to the manner." The very brief verse notes the people's burnt offering offered. The grammatical voices note the offering. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he brought the burnt offering, and offered it according to the manner" — Aaron offered the people's burnt offering according to the prescribed manner (Leviticus 1); the burnt offering (the people's whole devotion, after the atonement); the according to the manner (the prescribed ritual, the ordinance, Leviticus 1:3-9); the figure (the people's whole devotion, after the atonement; the ordered worship).

On "he brought the burnt offering," the witnesses note the burnt offering. Gill notes the people's burnt offering (the whole devotion, Leviticus 1) — the figure (the whole offering, the people's devotion).

On "offered it according to the manner," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the manner: "offered it according to the manner" — the prescribed ritual (the ordinance kept, Leviticus 1:3-9); the figure (the ordered worship, the obedience); the providence (the burnt offering offered as appointed). The verse displays the people's burnt offering offered.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offering — "he brought the burnt offering, and offered it according to the manner," the burnt offering (Leviticus 1, Keil, Gill); "he brought the burnt offering," the burnt offering (Gill); "offered it according to the manner," the manner (Leviticus 1:3-9, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the burnt offering. The verse marks the burnt offering: offered. The verse displays the manner: prescribed. The verse displays the devotion: whole. The verse displays the worship: ordered. The offering displays the people's whole devotion. The verse displays the offering: brought. The verse displays the manner: kept. The verse displays the worship: ordered. The people's burnt offering offered displays the whole devotion: he offered the burnt offering according to the manner (Leviticus 1:3-9) — the people's whole devotion offered in the prescribed manner, the ordered worship. The verse displays the meal offering and the morning sacrifice (v.17). That the burnt offering was offered 'according to the manner' shows that even in this first day of the priesthood's ministry nothing was left to invention, but the worship followed exactly the ordinance God had given, the whole devotion of the people rendered in the way he appointed.
17:
"And he brought the meat offering, and took an handful thereof, and burnt it upon the altar, beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning." The very brief verse notes the meal offering and the morning sacrifice. The grammatical voices note the meal offering. Ellicott and Gill note that "he brought the meat offering, and took an handful thereof, and burnt it upon the altar, beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning" — Aaron offered the meal offering (a handful burned), besides the daily morning burnt offering; the meal offering (the people's devotion, fine flour and oil, Leviticus 2); the handful (the memorial portion burned); the beside the morning sacrifice (the daily morning burnt offering, Exodus 29:38-39, the continual offering); the figure (the devotion offered with the continual sacrifice).

On "he brought the meat offering, and took an handful thereof," the witnesses note the meal offering. Gill notes the handful of the meal offering burned (the memorial, Leviticus 2:2) — the figure (the devotion presented).

On "beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the morning sacrifice: "beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning" — the daily morning burnt offering (the continual offering, Exodus 29:38-39); the figure (the continual worship, the daily sacrifice); the providence (the special offerings added to the daily, the continual worship). The verse displays the meal offering and the morning sacrifice.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the meal offering — "he brought the meat offering, and took an handful thereof, and burnt it upon the altar, beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning," the meal offering and morning sacrifice (Leviticus 2:2, Ellicott, Gill); "he brought the meat offering, and took an handful thereof," the meal offering (Gill); "beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning," the morning sacrifice (Exodus 29:38-39, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the meal offering. The verse marks the meal offering: the handful. The verse displays the morning sacrifice: the daily. The verse displays the devotion: offered. The verse displays the worship: continual. The offering displays the devotion with the continual sacrifice. The verse displays the meal offering: burned. The verse displays the morning: the daily. The verse displays the worship: continual. The meal offering and the morning sacrifice display the devotion with the continual worship: the meal offering, beside the morning sacrifice (Leviticus 2:2; Exodus 29:38-39) — the people's devotion offered with the daily morning burnt offering, the continual worship. The verse displays the peace offerings (vv.18-21).
18:
"He slew also the bullock and the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings, which was for the people: and Aaron's sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about," The very brief verse notes the slaying of the people's peace offerings. The grammatical voice notes the peace offerings. Gill notes that "he slew also the bullock and the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings, which was for the people: and Aaron's sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about" — Aaron slew the bullock and ram of the people's peace offerings, and sprinkled the blood on the altar; the peace offerings (the fellowship and communion with God, Leviticus 3); the slaying (the death of the victims); the blood sprinkled round about (the atonement applied); the figure (the peace offering, the fellowship with God, Romans 5:1; the communion of the people with God after the atonement).

On "he slew also the bullock and the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings," the witness notes the peace offerings. Gill notes the peace offerings slain (the fellowship offering, Leviticus 3) — the figure (the peace and communion with God).

On "the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about," the grammatical-historical note the blood: "the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about" — the blood sprinkled round about (the atonement applied, Leviticus 3:2); the figure (the peace through the blood, Colossians 1:20); the providence (the peace offerings, the fellowship after the atonement). The verse displays the slaying of the people's peace offerings.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witness agrees: the peace offerings — "he slew also the bullock and the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings... the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about," the peace offerings (Leviticus 3, Gill); "he slew also the bullock and the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings," the peace offerings (Romans 5:1, Gill); "the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about," the blood (Colossians 1:20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the peace offerings. The verse marks the peace offerings: slain. The verse displays the blood: sprinkled. The verse displays the fellowship: with God. The verse displays the Christ: our peace. The offerings display the fellowship with God. The verse displays the offerings: slain. The verse displays the blood: applied. The verse displays the peace: with God. The slaying of the people's peace offerings displays the fellowship: the peace offerings slain, the blood sprinkled (Leviticus 3; Romans 5:1; Colossians 1:20) — the people's fellowship and communion with God after the atonement, the figure of peace through Christ. The verse displays the fat and the wave breast (vv.19-21).
19:
"And the fat of the bullock and of the ram, the rump, and that which covereth the inwards, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver:" The very brief verse notes the fat of the peace offerings. The grammatical voices note the fat. Gill and Wesley note that "the fat of the bullock and of the ram, the rump, and that which covereth the inwards, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver" — the fat of the peace offerings (the rump, the fat of the inwards, the kidneys, the caul) was set apart to be burned; the fat (the best, reserved for God, Leviticus 3:9-10); the inward parts (the kidneys and caul, the seat of the affections); the figure (the best given to God, the inward devotion).

On "the fat of the bullock and of the ram, the rump," the witnesses note the fat. Gill notes the fat and the rump (the best, Leviticus 3:9) — the figure (the best, the inward, given to God).

On "and that which covereth the inwards, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver," the grammatical-historical note the inwards: "that which covereth the inwards, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver" — the inward parts (the seat of the affections, Leviticus 3:4); the figure (the inward devotion, the heart given to God); the providence (the fat reserved for God, to be burned, vv.20). The verse displays the fat of the peace offerings.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the fat — "the fat of the bullock and of the ram, the rump... and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver," the fat (Leviticus 3:9-10, Gill, Wesley); "the fat of the bullock and of the ram, the rump," the fat (Gill); "that which covereth the inwards, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver," the inwards (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fat. The verse marks the fat: reserved. The verse displays the rump: the choicest. The verse displays the inwards: the affections. The verse displays the best: to God. The fat displays the best given to God. The verse displays the fat: set apart. The verse displays the inward: offered. The verse displays the best: reserved. The fat of the peace offerings displays the best given to God: the fat, the rump, the inwards (Leviticus 3:9-10) — the choicest parts reserved for God, the figure of the inward devotion. The verse displays the fat burned and the breast waved (vv.20-21).
20:
"And they put the fat upon the breasts, and he burnt the fat upon the altar:" The very brief verse notes the fat burned upon the altar. The grammatical voice notes the fat. Gill notes that "they put the fat upon the breasts, and he burnt the fat upon the altar" — the fat was placed upon the breasts (to be waved, vv.21) and then burned on the altar; the fat upon the breasts (the fat laid on the breasts for the waving, Leviticus 7:30); the burned (the fat burned to God, the best given); the figure (the best given to God, the fat burned, the inward devotion offered).

On "they put the fat upon the breasts," the witness notes the placing. Gill notes the fat placed on the breasts (for the waving, Leviticus 7:30) — the order (the fat and breast presented).

On "he burnt the fat upon the altar," the grammatical-historical note the burning: "he burnt the fat upon the altar" — the fat burned to God (the best given, Leviticus 3:16); the figure (the best given to God, the inward devotion); the providence (the fat burned, the breast waved, vv.21). The verse displays the fat burned upon the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witness agrees: the fat — "they put the fat upon the breasts, and he burnt the fat upon the altar," the fat (Leviticus 7:30, Gill); "they put the fat upon the breasts," the placing (Gill); "he burnt the fat upon the altar," the burning (Leviticus 3:16, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the burned fat. The verse marks the fat: on the breasts. The verse displays the burning: on the altar. The verse displays the best: to God. The verse displays the devotion: inward. The fat displays the best given to God. The verse displays the fat: burned. The verse displays the breast: presented. The verse displays the best: reserved. The fat burned upon the altar displays the best given to God: the fat upon the breasts, burned on the altar (Leviticus 7:30; 3:16) — the best burned to God, the fat and breast presented, the inward devotion offered. The verse displays the wave breast (v.21).
21:
"And the breasts and the right shoulder Aaron waved for a wave offering before the LORD; as Moses commanded." The very brief verse notes the wave breast and heave shoulder waved before the LORD. The grammatical voices note the wave offering. JFB and Gill note that "the breasts and the right shoulder Aaron waved for a wave offering before the LORD; as Moses commanded" — Aaron waved the breasts and right shoulder of the peace offerings as a wave offering before the LORD, as commanded; the wave offering (the breast and shoulder moved before the LORD, dedicated to God and given to the priests, Leviticus 7:30-34); the as Moses commanded (the obedience); the figure (the priests' portion, the fellowship of the peace offering; the breast, love, and the shoulder, strength, dedicated to God and given to his ministers).

On "the breasts and the right shoulder Aaron waved for a wave offering," the witnesses note the wave offering. Gill notes the breast and shoulder waved (the priests' portion, Leviticus 7:30-34) — the figure (the priests' portion, the fellowship shared).

On "as Moses commanded," the grammatical-historical note the obedience: "as Moses commanded" — the obedience (the wave offering as commanded); the figure (the ordered worship); the providence (the wave breast and heave shoulder, the priests' portion, the fellowship). The verse displays the wave breast and heave shoulder waved before the LORD.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wave offering — "the breasts and the right shoulder Aaron waved for a wave offering before the LORD; as Moses commanded," the wave offering (Leviticus 7:30-34, JFB, Gill); "the breasts and the right shoulder Aaron waved for a wave offering," the wave offering (Gill); "as Moses commanded," the obedience (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wave offering. The verse marks the breast and shoulder: waved. The verse displays the dedication: to God. The verse displays the priests: their portion. The verse displays the obedience: as commanded. The wave offering displays the priests' portion and fellowship. The verse displays the breast: waved. The verse displays the shoulder: heaved. The verse displays the fellowship: shared. The wave breast and heave shoulder waved before the LORD display the priests' portion: the breast and shoulder waved, as commanded (Leviticus 7:30-34) — the priests' portion of the peace offering, dedicated to God and given to his ministers, the fellowship shared. The verse displays Aaron's blessing of the people (vv.22-24).
22:
"And Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them, and came down from offering of the sin offering, and the burnt offering, and peace offerings." The witnesses note Aaron lifting up his hand and blessing the people — the crux of the priestly blessing. On "Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them," interpreters expound the blessing. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the priestly blessing — "Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them": having finished the offerings, Aaron lifted his hands and blessed the people; the witnesses note the significance — the priestly blessing (the high priest blessing the people, the part of his office, the lifted hands, the benediction, probably the Aaronic blessing, "the LORD bless thee, and keep thee... the LORD make his face shine upon thee... the LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace," Numbers 6:23-27); the lesson — the priest's office to bless (the high priest blessing the people after the atonement, the blessing flowing from the finished sacrifice); the figure — Christ the great High Priest blessing his people, who at his ascension "lifted up his hands, and blessed them" (Luke 24:50-51, "he led them out as far as to Bethany, and he lifted up his hands, and blessed them; and... while he blessed them, he was parted from them, and carried up into heaven"); the blessing of the finished work, the benediction of the High Priest after the atonement; the figure of Christ's continual blessing of his people from the finished sacrifice.

On "and came down from offering of the sin offering, and the burnt offering, and peace offerings," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the finished offerings. Wesley and Henry note Aaron coming down from the offerings (the sin, burnt, and peace offerings finished) — the order (the blessing after the finished offerings); the figure (the blessing flowing from the completed sacrifice). The verse displays Aaron lifting up his hand and blessing the people.

The witnesses agree the substance: Aaron, the offerings finished, lifted his hands and blessed the people (Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the priestly blessing: "Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them" — the high priest's office to bless the people after the atonement (the Aaronic blessing, Numbers 6:23-27, the benediction flowing from the finished sacrifice), a figure of Christ the great High Priest who at his ascension lifted up his hands and blessed his people (Luke 24:50-51), the blessing of the finished work. The verse displays the priestly-blessing crux. The verse marks the hands: lifted up. The verse displays the blessing: pronounced. The verse displays the offerings: finished. The verse displays the Christ: blessing his people. The blessing displays the high priest's benediction after the atonement. The verse displays the Aaron: blessing. The verse displays the people: blessed. The verse displays the Christ: the great High Priest. Aaron lifting up his hand and blessing the people displays the crux: Aaron lifted up his hand and blessed them (Numbers 6:23-27; Luke 24:50-51) — the high priest's blessing after the atonement, a figure of Christ who lifted up his hands and blessed his people at his ascension. The verse displays the glory of the LORD appearing (vv.23-24).
23:
"And Moses and Aaron went into the tabernacle of the congregation, and came out, and blessed the people: and the glory of the LORD appeared unto all the people." The witnesses note Moses and Aaron blessing the people and the glory of the LORD appearing. On "Moses and Aaron went into the tabernacle... and came out, and blessed the people," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "Moses and Aaron went into the tabernacle of the congregation, and came out, and blessed the people: and the glory of the LORD appeared unto all the people" — Moses and Aaron went into the tabernacle (perhaps to present the offerings, or Moses to instruct Aaron in the inner service, or to pray), came out, and blessed the people, and then the glory of the LORD appeared; the going in and out (the entering and returning, the blessing); the joint blessing (Moses and Aaron together); the glory appearing (the promised glory, vv.6, now revealed to all the people).

On "Moses and Aaron... came out, and blessed the people," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the blessing. Calvin and Ellicott note Moses and Aaron blessing the people (the joint benediction, the law and the priesthood blessing) — the figure (the blessing of God's servants).

On "the glory of the LORD appeared unto all the people," interpreters expound the appearing. The dominant reading notes the glory revealed — the glory of the LORD appeared to all the people, fulfilling the promise (vv.6); the witnesses note the significance — the manifest presence of God, the glory revealed to all after the offerings and blessing (the visible glory, the shekinah, the token of God's acceptance and presence); the figure (the manifest glory after the finished worship, the presence of God revealed; the answer to the obedient worship, vv.6). The verse displays Moses and Aaron blessing the people and the glory of the LORD appearing.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses and Aaron blessed the people, and the glory of the LORD appeared to all (Keil, Calvin, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the glory of the LORD appeared — the manifest presence of God revealed to all the people after the offerings and the blessing (fulfilling the promise, vv.6), the visible glory the token of God's acceptance and presence, a figure of the manifest glory that crowns the finished and obedient worship. The verse displays the glory-appearing. The verse marks the blessing: of Moses and Aaron. The verse displays the glory: appeared. The verse displays the presence: manifest. The verse displays the acceptance: shown. The appearing displays the manifest glory after the worship. The verse displays the people: blessed. The verse displays the glory: revealed. The verse displays the presence: manifest. Moses and Aaron blessing the people and the glory of the LORD appearing display the manifest presence: they blessed the people, and the glory of the LORD appeared (vv.6) — the manifest glory of God revealed to all after the offerings and blessing, the token of his acceptance. The verse displays the fire from heaven (v.24).
24:
"And there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat: which when all the people saw, they shouted, and fell on their faces." The witnesses note the fire from the LORD consuming the offering — the crux of the divine acceptance. On "there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat," interpreters expound the fire. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the fire from heaven — "there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat": fire came out from the LORD (from the cloud, the glory) and consumed the offering on the altar; the witnesses note the significance — the divine acceptance of the sacrifice (the fire from heaven the visible token that God accepted the offering, consuming it, the sign of his acceptance and presence); the lesson — the fire from God the seal of acceptance (the sacrifice accepted, consumed by the divine fire, the answer of fire, as at other great moments, Genesis 15:17; Judges 6:21; 1 Kings 18:38, Elijah's offering; 1 Chronicles 21:26; 2 Chronicles 7:1, Solomon's temple; God answering by fire the sign of his acceptance); and this fire, once kindled from heaven, was to be kept ever burning (Leviticus 6:13), never replaced by strange fire (Leviticus 10:1); the figure — the divine acceptance of the sacrifice, ultimately of Christ's offering accepted by God (the resurrection the Father's seal of acceptance, Romans 1:4; Acts 2:24); the fire of God's holiness consuming the offering, the acceptance of the atonement.

On "which when all the people saw, they shouted, and fell on their faces," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the response. Wesley and Keil note the people's response — they shouted (with joy and praise) and fell on their faces (in awe and worship) — the joy and awe (the people rejoicing at the acceptance and falling in reverence at the manifest presence); the figure (the worship and joy at God's acceptance and glory). The verse displays the fire from the LORD consuming the offering.

The witnesses agree the substance: fire came from the LORD and consumed the offering, and the people shouted and fell on their faces (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the divine acceptance: "there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed... the burnt offering" — the fire from heaven the visible token of God's acceptance of the sacrifice (the answer of fire, as Genesis 15:17; 1 Kings 18:38; 2 Chronicles 7:1), the fire of God's holiness consuming the offering, kept ever burning thereafter (Leviticus 6:13), a figure of the divine acceptance of the atonement and ultimately of Christ's offering accepted by God; and the people's response of joy and awe (the shout and the falling on their faces) the right response to God's manifest acceptance and glory. The verse displays the divine-acceptance crux. The verse marks the fire: from the LORD. The verse displays the offering: consumed and accepted. The fire displays the divine acceptance of the sacrifice, the people's joy and awe the right response. The fire from the LORD consuming the offering displays the crux: fire came from before the LORD and consumed the offering, and the people shouted and fell on their faces (Genesis 15:17; 1 Kings 18:38; 2 Chronicles 7:1) — the divine acceptance of the sacrifice sealed by the fire of heaven, a figure of the acceptance of the atonement, the people's joy and awe the right response. The verse closes the chapter, before the strange fire of Nadab and Abihu (ch.10).
